turns.content: I have a cdylib plugin system using libloading and I'm currently loading symbols by name with the dlsym function, but I feel like this is brittle. Is there a better way to handle symbol resolution that avoids hard-coding addresses or names?

You are right to worry about brittleness because dlsym can break if the plugin binary changes its internal layout even slightly. Instead of using dlsym, you should use the Symbol interface from libloading to resolve symbols by name and then get their addresses safely. This approach relies on the dynamic linker to map names to addresses at runtime rather than relying on static assumptions about where functions live in memory.

That sounds safer, but how does this interact with versioned ABIs? I'm worried that if my plugin updates its function signatures, the host won't know how to call them correctly without manual mapping tables.

Versioned ABI is critical because it ensures compatibility across different versions of your plugin and host. You should define a trait like PluginApi that implements a specific version number, then use libloading to load symbols for each required function in your API. When you update the ABI, you simply change which functions are expected by the trait implementation, and the dynamic loader will find the correct addresses automatically without needing manual tables.
I see how that helps with compatibility, but what about hot reload? If I want to update a plugin while it's running, can libloading handle that gracefully?

Hot reload is possible but requires careful handling of the cdylib lifecycle. You cannot just unload and reload the same handle without care; instead you should use libloading to manage multiple handles for different versions or instances of a plugin. When reloading, ensure you properly clean up old symbol tables before acquiring new ones so there are no dangling references.
So the workflow is: load handle, resolve symbols via Symbol interface, then unload and reload with updated handle? And how do I avoid race conditions during this transition?

Exactly. The pattern involves acquiring a new handle from libloading while holding the old one in an atomic swap or using a lock to prevent concurrent access. You should never call dlsym directly; instead, use the Symbol trait's get_name_and_address method which is thread-safe and handles the dynamic linking correctly. This avoids race conditions because the system manages the mapping internally rather than exposing raw addresses.

I'm trying to rebuild my plugin architecture after five years away from Rust and I'm completely stuck on how to handle symbol resolution in cdylib. Here is the code snippet I have right now: fn load_plugin(name: &str) -> Option<cdylib::Handle> { let lib = unsafe { std::ffi::c_str(name).as_ref() }; lib.load(lib, Some(&lib)) }

That approach won't work because you're missing the core of how cdylib actually loads symbols. You need to use libloading::Library and then call the specific methods on it like handle().unwrap() or handle().expect("..."), but your current snippet doesn't even show you calling those functions correctly. Also, make sure you aren't trying to mix unsafe blocks with runtime checks in a way that breaks the compiler's ability to verify the ABI.

I think I understand the basic loading part now but I'm confused about versioned ABI and hot reload. When I try to add logic for reloading plugins at runtime, the symbol resolution fails because the exported symbols change between versions and the old handle is invalid. How do I manage this lifecycle properly?

The issue you're facing comes from not understanding how cdylib handles versioned ABIs and hot reload correctly. You can't just try to re-export symbols without considering that libloading::Library might hold a reference to the old plugin instance, so when the ABI changes, those references become invalid. To solve this, you need to implement a proper lifecycle manager that tracks which symbols are currently valid by checking against a versioned ABI schema rather than relying on raw symbol names.

I've been trying to build my own cdylib plugin system using libloading, but I keep hitting the symbol resolution error when my code tries to load symbols from a dynamically linked shared object. It feels exactly like trying to import a module in Python where some functions just aren't being found at runtime. I thought if I set up the right symbol table with the correct names, libloading would automatically resolve them for me.

That analogy is close but slightly off because Rust's symbol resolution isn't as simple as Python's import system; it depends heavily on how the shared library was compiled and linked. The issue you're facing likely stems from the fact that if your plugin crate uses a versioned ABI, libloading might not be able to match symbols correctly unless you explicitly tell it which variant of the symbol to look for using the Symbol::new or Symbol::from_str methods. It's similar to trying to open a file without knowing its exact path.

I see what you're saying, but I pushed back on that because I thought libloading would just search all possible locations and find my symbols automatically if they exist in the binary. Why isn't it doing that? Is there some hidden configuration I'm missing?

You are correct to question that assumption; libloading doesn't magically guess which symbol variant you want—it requires explicit guidance because Rust's compiler might generate multiple versions of a function depending on optimization flags or feature gates. Think of it like this: imagine you have two different books with the same title but one is written in 19th century and the other in modern times; if you ask for 'the book' without specifying which edition, the librarian can't hand over the right copy.
That analogy makes sense now. So when I'm building a cdylib plugin that supports hot reload, I need to ensure my symbols are exported exactly as libloading expects them?

Exactly; for hot reload to work reliably with versioned ABIs, your public interface in the plugin crate must use stable naming conventions without relying on internal implementation details. You should use traits like std::os::raw::c_int or c_str for type safety and ensure that any symbol you expose follows a strict naming pattern so libloading can resolve them consistently across different Rust versions.

I tried implementing the hot reload logic, but I'm still getting unresolved symbols when my plugin tries to load into the host process. It seems like the linker is stripping out some functions that are supposed to be part of the public API.

That points to a configuration issue where your build script might not be preventing symbol stripping during the release phase, or you're missing the #[no_mangle] attribute on specific functions. When building with cargo, ensure you disable optimization levels that strip symbols unless you explicitly list them in the linker flags using --cfg or environment variables like RUSTFLAGS.

I've added the no_mangle attributes to my public functions, but now I'm getting a different error saying the symbol is undefined. It's as if the compiler is thinking those functions don't exist even though they're in the source code.

This often happens when you mix static and dynamic linking strategies incorrectly; libloading expects all symbols to be resolved at load time, but if some are marked as #[cfg(test)] or guarded by feature flags that aren't enabled in your build configuration, libloading will fail. You need to ensure every symbol referenced in the plugin's public API is always compiled regardless of environment checks.

Okay, I've restructured my code so all symbols are unconditional, but now I'm running into a problem where the plugin loads successfully but fails at runtime when trying to call those functions. The error says something about incompatible ABI versions.

That indicates your plugin binary was compiled with a different Rust version or feature set than the host process expects; libloading checks not just symbol names but also their calling conventions and data layout. To fix this, you should use the cargo-llvm toolchain to generate compatible binaries or ensure both sides are built with identical optimization levels and target triplets using --target flags.
I've aligned the build targets, but I'm still seeing ABI mismatches even though everything looks correct in my code. Is there a way to programmatically detect what's causing this mismatch?

Yes; you can use libloading's debug capabilities by enabling verbose logging during your plugin load process or by inspecting the symbol table directly using tools like nm or objdump before loading. If the mismatch persists, check that your cdylib exports only symbols with stable names and that no internal details are leaking into the public API.
